IN THE HIGH COURT OF LESOTHO
(COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
HELD AT MASERU                                                              CCT/0376/2020
In the matter between:
FANTASTIC CLOTHING (PTY) LTD                                 APPLICANT
AND
LUQY’S GROUP INVESTMENT (PTY) LTD                      RESPONDENT
Neutral Citation:
Fantastic Clothing (Pty) Ltd v Luqy’s Group Investment (Pty) Ltd
[2026] LSHC 15
Comm. (05 FEBRUARY 2026)
CORAM:             MOKHESI J
HEARD:               04 DECEMBER 2025
DELIVERED:     05 FEBRUARY 2026
SUMMARY
Law of Costs:
Review of taxation
-
The taxing master brought his decision on taxation on review based on the provisions of rule 49 – The applicant had objected to the taxing master’s ruling allowing three items on the bill of costs pertaining to fees of counsel described as disbursements – Held, the taxing master did not determine the reasonableness of the fees- Matter remitted back to him to deal with the items in line with the principles articulated in the judgment.
ANNOTATIONS
Legislation:
High Court Rules 1980
High Court Civil Litigation Rules, 2024
Books:
Rochelle Francis-Subbiah
Taxation of Legal Costs in South Africa 1
st
ed.

Lesotho
Moshebi and Others v Select Management Services LAC (2013-2014) 265
PT Ratalane Construction (Pty) Ltd v Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security
[2024] LSHC 251
Comm. (11 December 2024)
South Africa
Aircraft Completions Centre (Pty) Ltd v Rassouw and Others 2004 (1) SA 123 (W)
Bowman NO v Avraamides & Another 1991(1) SA 92 (W)
Brener NO v Sonnenberg, Murphy, Leo Burnett (Pty) Ltd 1999 (4) SA 503 (W)
Coetzee v Taxing Master, South Gauteng High Court) 2013(1) SA 74 (GSJ)
Curtis v Johannesburg Municipality 1906 TS 308
Gross v Svirsky 1923 TPD 422
Hawkins v Gelb and Another 1959 (1) SA 702 (WLD)
Kruger v President Insurance Co. Ltd 1994 (2) SA 495 (DCLD)
Maseka v Law Society of the Northern Province (443/06)
[2010] ZANWHC 13
(1 January 2010)
Mkuyana v Road Accident Fund 2020(6) SA 405 (ECG)
Mouton and Another v Martine 1968 (4) SA 738 (T) 738
Natal Joint Municipal Pension Fund v Endumeni Municipality 2012 (4) SA 593 (SCA)
President of the Republic of South Africa and Others v Gauteng Lions Rugby Union 2002(2) SA 64 (CC)
RH Christie Incorporated v Taxing Master – Supreme Court of Appeal (1086/2018)
[2021] ZASCA 152
(27 October 2021
S v Mhlungu and Others [1995] ZACC4; 1995 (3) SA 867
Shidiack v Union Government, 1912 AD
Texas Co (SA) Ltd v Cape Town Municipality 1926 AD 467
Trollip v Taxing Mistress of the High Court 2018 (6) SA 292 (ECG)
Wellworths Bazaars Ltd v Chandlers Ltd 1947 (4) SA 453 (T)
Judgment on Review of Taxation.
[1]
Introduction.
This is a review of taxation ruling made by the taxing master in terms of the provisions of rule 186(1) of the High Court Civil Litigation Rules 2024. The applicant called on the taxing master in terms of this rule to state a case for the decision by this court.  This rule provides that a party dissatisfied with the ruling of the taxing master as to any item or part of the bill of costs which was objected to or disallowed
mero motu
by the taxing master, may within fourteen days after the allocatur is issued, require the taxing master to state a case for the decision by a judge. The applicant who is the 1
st
respondent in the main case, lodged the review against an allocatur dated 22 August 2025 regarding three items characterized as disbursements, namely:
Item 9 – M200,000.00 (Two Hundred Thousand Maloti) – with particulars: “Perusal 1
st
part-payment from client (professional fees).”
Item 20 – M100,000.00 (One Hundred Thousand Maloti) – with particulars: “Perusal of 2
nd
part-payment from client (professional fee).”
Item 35 – M250,000.00 (Two Hundred and Fifty Thousand Maloti) – with particulars: “Perusal of 3
rd
part-payment from client (professional fee).”
[2]     The grounds of objection were as stated as follows:
“1. Paragraph 17(b) of the judgment explicitly stated that there shall be payment of 50% of the costs at the attorney and client scale.  The allocatur does not factor this.
2. The impugned items in the allocatur were granted as a fait accompli and in the exercise of discretion to allow taxing master must assess the following:
Complexity of the matter
Experience of the legal practitioner
Whether the legal practitioner is an attorney or counsel
The tariffs prescribed by law.
3. None of the factors expressed in paragraph 2 above were factored in allowing the “disbursements” and a consequent amount to the tune of M550,000.00 (Five Hundred and Fifty Thousand Maloti) in toto.  None of the items qualify as disbursements as alleged or at all.”
[3]
Background Facts.
Taxation does not occur in a vacuum. It is always coloured by or steeped in the factual circumstances of a case to which a bill relates. It is therefore necessary that this factual context be examined to gain a proper perspective of the bill in issue.  Litigation history between these parties is quite a chequered one, but it is quite straightforward to understand. The parties will be referred to as they are in the present review application or by their names. Fantastic clothing (the applicant) was the 1
st
and the main defendant in a case which was initiated by way of summons by Luqy’s Group (the respondent) claiming the sum M1,330,000.00 as refund of the purchase price paid to Fantastic Clothing, and other reliefs.
[4]     The parties had entered into a sale agreement around February 2020, in terms of which Luqy’s Group purchased a M1,330,000.00 worth of fabric from the applicant.  Luqy’s Group paid M650,000.00, M350,000.00 and M330,000.00 respectively towards the purchase price.  The fabric was duly collected by Luqy’s Group. Disagreement between the parties erupted due to what Lugy’s Group considered to be a substandard fabric supplied to it by Fantastic Clothing.  It, as a result of these complaints, instituted proceedings on 06 October 2020 in CCT/0376/2020.  But before instituting the above proceedings, on 18 August 2020, it instituted motion proceedings in CCA/0090/2020 aimed at “founding jurisdiction” on the ground that Fantastic was relocating to South Africa.
[5]     As a result of institution of the case in CCA/0090/2020 substantial quantity of fabric was attached and other property was removed from Fantastic Clothing’s premises because it did not contest the application.  The summons which was issued in CCT/0376/2020 was not contested and, consequently default judgment was granted by Mahase J against Fantastic Clothing on 19 November 2020 and the writ of execution was issued on the same date.  On 13 November 2020 Fantastic Clothing filed an application for rescission of the said default judgment and was successful in the result.  Judgment was rendered on 26 February 2024.  Fantastic Clothing unsuccessfully appealed against my order granting rescission.
[6]     Following the above unsuccessful appeal against my order granting rescission, Luqy’s Group launched a mareva-type interdict application against Fantastic Clothing, and this application’s bill of costs is the one at the centre of the present review of taxation.  In the preceding paragraphs I mentioned that Fantastic Clothing was granted judgment by default by Mahase J and writ of execution was issued immediately, and that in the process, Fantastic Clothing’s fabric and other property were seized.  When Fantastic Clothing applied for rescission, the matter first served before the Learned Chief Justice and Luqy’s Group insisted on payment of security in exchange for the return of the goods in its possession.
[7]     A settlement agreement was reached between the parties which was made an order of court by the Chief Justice on 16 November 2020.  In that order he stayed the execution of judgment and directed Fantastic Clothing to “furnish security in the sum of Two Million Maloti (M2,000,000.00) in the form of surety bond to be filed with the Registrar by the Applicant’s Legal Representatives”.  Fantastic Clothing’s legal counsel, Advocate Phafane KC, provided the surety bond as ordered after Fantastic Clothing had deposited an amount of M2,000,000.00 into his bank account.  For the reason that Fantastic had closed down and has no commercial footprint in Lesotho – incidentally its Chinese shareholders had left the country and gone elsewhere – and the fact that the amount of money mentioned in the preceding paragraph, its only asset in Lesotho, was held in a bank account of an advocate not in an attorney’s trust account, prompted Luqy’s Group to lodge the above mentioned Mareva-type interdict and sought further to have the money removed from the bank account of Advocate Phafane K.C and same be held in trust by its attorney. Luqy’s was successful in this application. Its application for leave to appeal the rescission order – which was heard together with the application for an interdict- was unsuccessful due to non-compliance with the rules. Regarding the interdict, I awarded Luqy’s Group costs on attorney and client scale at 50% per the parties’ agreement.  Fantastic Clothing unsuccessfully appealed against my order granting interdict against it.  These are the facts which form the context of the bill of costs in this matter.  I turn to deal with the law on review of taxation, but before I do that, I deal with the two important procedural issues which seem not to have been appreciated by the taxing master in this case,
viz,
the form of stated case and his report.
[8]
The law.
Stated case and report of the taxing master.
Rule 186 of the High Court Civil Litigation Rule 2024 provides:
“(1) A party dissatisfied with the ruling of the taxing master as to any item or part thereof which was objected to or disallowed mero motu by the taxing master may, within fourteen days after the allocatur is issued, require the taxing master to state a case for the decision before a Judge.
(2) The case referred to in sub-rule (1) shall set out each item or part thereof together with the grounds of objection advanced at the taxation and shall include any finding of fact by the taxing master.
(3) A case may not be stated under sub-rule (1) where the amount or the total of the amounts which the taxing master has disallowed and which the party dissatisfied seeks to have allowed or disallowed respectively is less than M5,000.00 unless the taxing master consents to the stating of the case.
(4) The taxing master shall supply a copy of the stated case to each of the parties who may, within ten days after receipt thereof, submit their contentions in writing including the grounds of objection not advanced at the taxation, in respect of any item or part thereof which was objected to before the taxing master or disallowed mero motu by the taxing master.
(5) On receipt of the contentions referred to in sub-rule (4), the taxing master shall compile his report and shall supply a copy thereof to each of the parties who may, within ten days after receipt of that report, submit their further contentions if any, in writing to the taxing master.
(6) On receipt of the parties’ contentions in terms of sub-rule (5), the taxing master shall without delay lay the case together with the contentions of the parties and his report and any further contentions thereon before a Judge.
(7) On receipt of a case submitted to him under sub-rule (6), the Judge may decide the matter –
(a) on the case and contentions so submitted together with any further information which he may require from the taxing master; or
(b) after a hearing in his chambers if he considers it appropriate.
(8) On receipt of the submissions referred to in sub-rule (4), the taxing master may, instead of compiling a report as contemplated in sub-rule (5) refer the case for decision to the court and any further information to be supplied by the taxing master to the Judge and such information shall also be supplied to the parties who may within fourteen days after receipt thereof submit contentions in writing thereon to the taxing master.
(9) On receipt of the parties’ contentions in terms of sub-rule (8), the taxing master shall without delay lay such further information together with any contentions of the parties before the Judge.
(10) The Judge deciding a case referred in terms of this rule may make such order as to costs of suit as he considers just and fair, including an order that the unsuccessful party shall pay to the opposing party a sum fixed by the Judge as to costs.”
[9]     Persuasive guidance on these matters where the court was dealing with a similar South African rule, was provided in
Brener NO v Sonnenberg, Murphy, Leo Burnett (Pty) Ltd 1999 (4) SA 503 (W)
at 508B thus:
“As was pointed out by Schutz J in Nedperm Bank Ltd v Desbie (Pty) Ltd 1995 (2) SA 711 (W) at 713A-C, when the Taxing Master initially states a case in terms of Rule 48, he is not required to 'write an essay'. All he must do is, as required by the Rule, to 'set out each item or part of an item together with the grounds of objection advanced at the taxation and . . . any finding of facts by the Taxing Master'. After that the parties are to deliver to him their contentions in writing. It is only at that stage that the Taxing Master is called upon to prepare a 'report'. It is his 'report', to be made in the light of the parties' written contentions, which is 'the occasion to give his reasons in full'. After that the parties have the last word in further contentions dealing with the Taxing Master's report. Despite the fact that Rule 48 makes provision for these exchanges after the Taxing Master has stated a case, it is important to bear in mind that the stated case remains the foundation of the parties' initial contentions, of the Taxing Master's report and of the parties' further contentions that are to follow in terms of the Rule. The stated case, if not initially prepared with due care, is likely to create problems that complicate, or even frustrate, the steps in the review proceeding that have still to follow. The importance of proper compliance with the requirement that the stated case 'shall embody any finding of facts by the Taxing Master' was emphasised in Cordingley NO v BP Southern Africa (Pty) Ltd 1971 (3) SA 118 (O) at 122B-C. That was a case which dealt with the equivalent Rule in the magistrates' courts. In Kruger v Sekretaris van Binnelandse Inkomste 1973 (3) SA 231 (A) at 232E-233A Rabie JA, dealing with the equivalent Rule in the Appellate Division, emphasised the importance of stating the case properly at the outset, and some of the problems which can result if the requirements of the Rule are ignored or dealt with in an imprecise or slipshod manner.”
[10]   In the present case the taxing master merely provided a document which on the face of it appears to double up as a stated case and a report because in it he deals with the case law which he states backs up his position to allow the objected items in the bill of costs.  He does not make any finding of fact regarding the objected items. He clearly did not follow the above stated guideline as provided in rule 186.
[11]
Principles regarding review of taxation decision by the taxing master.
The purpose of taxation in the law of costs, firstly, is to fix costs at the certain amount for purposes of execution, and secondly, to ensure that the party who is ordered to pay costs does not pay excessively, and the successful litigant does not receive insufficient costs (
Mouton and Another v Martine 1968 (4) SA 738 (T) 738
at 742).  The function of taxing bill of costs is bestowed on the taxing master by the rules of this court.  Taxation seeks to determine whether fees charged by attorneys are reasonable.  In attorney and client bill of costs, reasonableness of the fees is a predominant criterion. As was stated in
President of the Republic of South Africa and Others v Gauteng Lions Rugby Union 2002(2) SA 64 (CC)
para. [51]
“[t]he question is whether it would be fair to both sides to allow recoupment of virtually the whole of very substantial attorney and client charges in respect of counsel’s fees.”
[12]   The taxing masters exercise public power when they do their work and as such they must do so within the bounds of the empowering law
(
Coetzee v Taxing Master, South Gauteng High Court) 2013(1) SA 74 (GSJ)
para. [8]).  They exercise a discretion when taxing the bill of costs. It is for this reason that the courts do not readily interfere with the exercise of such a discretion except where it has not been exercised properly, for example he acted
mala fide
; or from ulterior purpose or motives; or has not applied his mind to the matter or exercised his discretion at all; or if he disregard regulatory commands (
RH Christie Incorporated v Taxing Master – Supreme Court of Appeal (1086/2018)
[2021] ZASCA 152
(27 October 2021:
see also
Shidiack v Union Government, 1912 AD
at p. 651
).
The court is however not restricted to these grounds of review as it exercises wide supervision over the exercise of discretion by the taxing masters in terms of rule 186.  The court will interfere or correct the taxing master’s ruling when it is satisfied that he was clearly wrong (
Moshebi and Others v Select Management Services LAC (2013-2014) 265
at 267 (para.[8]).  This principle was articulated in the case of
Wellworths Bazaars Ltd v Chandlers Ltd 1947 (4) SA 453 (T)
at 458
thus dealing with the
general principle stated in the preceding sentences:
“…That is the general principle.  But principle has had engrafted upon it something else, and that is this:  There is a certain class of case where the point on which the court is able to form as good an opinion as the Taxing Master and perhaps, even better opinion.  Examples of that class of case are these: First of all, the question whether the employment of more than one counsel was justified in the case.  It is apparent that a Judge is in a better position to decide such a matter than the Taxing could be.  Another example is when the relevancy of evidence arise.  The Taxing Master may disallow the costs of an affidavit that the affidavit was not relevant to any issue in the case.  It is clear that the Judge must be in a better position to decide that point than the Taxing Master …”
[13]   It is important that I get out of the way certain preliminary issues before I delve into the nub of this review, namely, which rules are applicable to the present review between the new and the repealed rules; location of the items in – professional fees- issue characterised as disbursements, and whether the conceptual distinction between attorney and client costs and attorney and own client costs matters during taxation. I turn to deal with these issues:
[14]
Location of counsel professional fees in the bill of costs: when to place under the ‘Fees’ column or ‘disbursements’ column on the bill of costs.
An issue arose regarding he location of fees payable to counsel who argued the matter.  On the bill of costs, fees in the amount of M55,000.00 represented by Items 09 of 26/02/2024; Item 20 of 18/03/2024 and Item 35 of 24/05/2024 represented the amounts of M200,000.00, M100,000.00 and M250,000.00 respectively.  These amounts were characterised as disbursements instead of fees. Proofs of payments in the amount of M397,840.00 instead of M550,000.00, ironically, were produced before the taxing master.  Although it may seem straightforward, in this case much debate was generated around what disbursements are in the bill of costs.  Disbursements and expenses are not attorney’s fees, but the actual costs incurred by the attorney to third parties to enable the rendering of professional service on behalf of the client.  On this point I reference what the learned author Rochelle Francis-Subbiah
Taxation of Legal Costs in South Africa 1
st
ed.,
stated at
p. 121:
“Disbursement and expenses are not attorney’s fees but the actual monies paid by an attorney to a third party to enable the rendering of professional service on behalf of a client.  These costs are specifically represented in a separate column from the attorney’s fees in a bill of costs.  Expenses incurred by the attorney include telephone call, stamps, courier expenses, photo-copying, sheriff fees, copies of police reports, transcripts of court records, transcript of evidence, counsel fees, medico-legal reports etc.
It is essential to determine the necessity of a disbursement when allowing it.  The Taxing Master has a discretion to go outside the strict terms of the tariff as the tariff is not exhaustive regarding incidental expenses or disbursement, provided the necessity of the expenses is shown …  Proof of actual expenditure will be required when it is dispute or required by the taxing master.  An attorney cannot charge for disbursement without being able to justify these amounts…”
[15]   The fees of attorney functioning as counsel or counsel’s fees may be charged as disbursement provided counsel or attorney functioning as such issued an account to instructing attorneys who are liable to settling it, otherwise in the absence of this account, those costs should be included in the fees column of the bill at the rate under the tariff of fees for advocates under rule 55
(cf.  Maseka v Law Society of the Northern Province (443/06)
[2010] ZANWHC 13
(1 January 2010)
at para. 61
)
.  Incidentally rule 55(3) provides that for an attorney to claim fees disbursed to advocate he/she must produce to the taxing master the marked brief of the advocate in each case.  In the present case counsel’s fees were justified on the basis of proofs of payments with no accompanying advocate’s account.  In the absence of the advocate’s account, the fees disbursed to counsel ought to have been included in the fees column accompanied by marked brief of the advocate.
[16]
Which rules are applicable in the present case?
Advocate Makara for the respondent argued, based on rule 195 of the High Court Civil Litigation Rules 2024 (hereinafter, ‘the new rules’) that the applicable tariffs are the ones in the new rules not the repealed 1980 rules (the ‘old rules’).  Mr Rasekoai, for the applicant, contended that the new rules are not retrospective, especially their tariffs aspects, because those are not mere procedural provisions as they affect the rights of the parties to matters which were instituted before the coming into effect of the new rules in June 2024.
[17]   Rule 195 provides:
“(1) Despite the repeal of the rules listed under rule 196:
anything done under the repealed rules and when could have been done under a corresponding rule of these rules, is deemed to have been done under such corresponding rule;
a case that has been filed in the registry or has been allocated to a presiding Judge under the repealed rules continues under these rules, but if there is any uncertainty in this regard, the presiding Judge may direct the appropriate procedure to be followed after considering representation from the parties;…”
[18]   The presumption against retrospectivity of legislation is an aid to interpretation of statutes. The intention of the legislature, therefore, assumes paramountcy when a particular piece of legislation is interpreted (
Kruger v President Insurance Co. Ltd 1994 (2) SA 495 (DCLD)
at 503).  The well-known principles of interpretation stand to be applied, namely, the triad of considerations, namely, the context, by reading the particular provision in the light of the whole document; language used in the provision and the purpose to which the provision is directed (
Natal Joint Municipal Pension Fund v Endumeni Municipality 2012 (4) SA 593 (SCA)
at para. [18].
[19]   Rule 195 is a savings and transitional provision of the new rules. It serves two purposes; to regulate the coming into effect of the new rules and to bring certainty regarding things done under the repealed rules in terms of clarifying whether they continue to be dealt with under those repealed rules or the new rules.  It uses a deeming provision because things done under the old rules cannot be said to be done under the new rules.  This is the context and purpose in which this provision was inserted in the new rules.  This rule should also be seen in light of an exception to the rule of construction of statutes that statutes should be construed as not having a retrospective effect unless contrary intention of the legislature is clearly expressed. The exception to this rule relates to matters dealing with procedure, as Innes CJ states in
Curtis v Johannesburg Municipality 1906 TS 308
at 311 and 312:
'The general rule is that, in the absence of express provision to the contrary, statutes should be considered as affecting future matters only. . . . Every law regulating legal procedure must, in the absence of express provision to the contrary, necessarily govern, so far as it is applicable the procedure in every suit which comes to trial after the date of its promulgation. Its prospective operation would not be complete if this were not so, and it must regulate all such procedure even though the cause of action arose before the date of promulgation and even though the suit may have been then pending.'
[20]   To determine whether rules are retrospective in light of whether they are purely procedural in nature is not decisive. One would tend to think that
since the rules of court by and large deal with procedural matters the lawgiver would probably have thought that they are benign in effect on the parties involved in matters which predate them, but as I illustrate below, that is not always the case. They may be procedural but affect vested rights of the litigants to a case. The example is the case of
PT Ratalane Construction (Pty) Ltd v Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security [2024] LSHC 251 Comm. (11 December 2024)
where in the context of summary judgment procedure, the court made a point that even though rule 195 states that whatever was done under the old rules will be taken to be done under the corresponding rule of the new rules, it created problems in that case for the following reasons; though rule 137 of the new rules and rule 28 of the old rules are
in pari materia,
the former
reformed the latter in a critical manner
.
The new rules overhauled how summary judgment applications should be dealt with.  Such an application in terms of the old rules could be lodged without the plaintiff having had sight of the defendant’s plea.  But in the new rules, summary judgment application can only be lodged once an answer has been filed. Had that application which had been lodged under the old rules been dealt with in terms of the new rules it would have meant that it should have been dismissed for being irregular in terms of the new rules even though it was perfectly lodged in conformity with the old rules.  To avert this eventuality, I applied the old rules, because not applying them would have been prejudicial to the plaintiff.  It would have affected its vested right to have its application succeed in terms of the old rules under which it was lodged and was fully compliant.
[21]   As I understand the context and purpose of rule 195, the new rules are retrospective in effect. But inasmuch it mandates retrospectivity of the new rules, I do not read it to be mandating their application to all cases that precedes them, because where they affect vested rights, they should not be resorted to. The approach to these matters was articulated in
S v Mhlungu and Others [1995] ZACC4; 1995 (3) SA
867
at paras. [65]-[67]:
“[65] First, there is a strong presumption that new legislation is not intended to be retroactive. By retroactive legislation is meant legislation which invalidates what was previously valid, or vice versa, ie which affects transactions completed before the new statute came into operation. See Van Lear v Van Lear (supra). It is legislation which enacts that 'as at a past date the law shall be taken to have been that which it was not'. See Shewan Tomes & Co Ltd v Commissioner of Customs and Excise 1955 (4) SA 305 (A) at 311H, per Schreiner ACJ. There is also a presumption against reading legislation as being retrospective in the sense that, while it takes effect only from its date of commencement, it impairs existing rights and obligations, eg by invalidating current contracts or impairing existing property rights. See Cape Town Municipality v F Robb & Co Ltd 1966 (4) SA 345 (C) at 351, per Corbett J. The general rule therefore is that a statute is as far as possible to be construed as operating only on facts which come into existence after its passing.
[66] There is a different presumption where a new law effects changes in procedure. It is presumed that such a law will apply to every case subsequently tried 'no matter when such case began or when the cause of action arose' - Curtis v Johannesburg Municipality 1906 TS 308 at 312. It is, however, not always easy to decide whether a new statutory provision is purely procedural or whether it also affects substantive rights. Rather than categorising new provisions in this way, it has been suggested, one should simply ask whether or not they would affect vested rights if applied retrospectively. See Yew Bon Tew v Kenderaan Bas Mara (supra at 563 (AC)); Industrial Council for Furniture Manufacturing Industry, Natal v Minister of Manpower and Another (supra at 242).
[67] There is still another well-established rule of construction namely, 1995 (3) SA p898 KENTRIDGE AJ that even if a new statute is intended to be retrospective insofar as it affects vested rights and obligations, it is nonetheless presumed not to affect matters which are the subject of pending legal proceedings. See Bell v Voorsitter van die Rasklassifikasieraad en Andere (supra); Bellairs v Hodnett and Another (supra at 1148).
[22]   If the rules are applied in an all-or-nothing manner, they would have a devastating effect on vested rights of the parties involved, particularly the losing party.  A clear example is the present case where the new rules exponentially revised fee tariffs of advocates and attorneys and other matters.  If this matter is dealt with in terms of the new rules it would mean that the cost debtor would pay more cost than it would have under the old rules. The result is that the new rules should not be resorted to as regards the applicable tariffs. The tariffs in the new rules are, therefore, inapplicable to the present matter.
[23]
Is there a practical distinction between an award of costs as between attorney and client and own client scales?
In his submissions, Mr Rasekoai, for the applicant, contended that the approach adopted by the taxing master in the present case in allowing disbursement as professional fees in the amounts mentioned above, exemplifies his lack of appreciation on the distinction between costs on attorney and client scale and attorney and own client scale. I do not the share a view that the taxing master displayed lack of appreciation of the distinction between the award of costs on these scales, but rather, as showing lack of appreciation on how to treat them on taxation.
[24]   Rule 56(1) empowers the taxing master to tax “
all bills of costs actually rendered by an attorney in his capacity as such, whether or not in connection with litigation, provided that the taxing master shall not tax costs in instances where some other official is empowered by statute to do so.”
Implicit in this sub-rule is that the taxing master is empowered to tax bills of costs between parties to litigation and between attorney and his client. These two bills serve different purposes. The former is aimed at providing adequate indemnity to a successful party, while the latter is aimed at a client making a reasonable recompense for services rendered to him by his attorney. I am using the words
‘adequate indemnity’
because rule 56 is not meant to provide 100% indemnity to the successful party. Thus, in
Texas Co (SA) Ltd v Cape Town Municipality 1926 AD 467
at 488 it was said:
“Now costs are awarded to a successful party in order to indemnify him for the expense to which he has been put through having been unjustly compelled either to initiate or to defend litigation as the case may be. Owing to the necessary operation of taxation, such an award is seldom a complete indemnity; but that does not affect the principle on which it is based. Speaking generally, only amounts which the suitor has paid, or becomes liable to pay in connection with the due presentment of his case are recoverable as costs.”
[25]   Even though this rule is not meant to provide complete indemnity to the winning party, it is deemed to do so:
“[A] Court, must operate on the assumption that Rule 70(3) [for present purposes rule 56(5)] does achieve what it sets out to achieve. It is a Rule which determines the taxation of party and party costs. It not only authorises but requires that its injunction shall be applied with a specific object. The object is that the party to whom costs are awarded is afforded 'full indemnity' for every expenditure 'reasonably incurred by him in relation to his claim or defence'. It is expressly added that the object is also to ensure that 'all such costs' shall be borne by the party against whom the order has been awarded. In order to achieve those objects the Taxing Master must allow all costs, charges and expenses which appear to him to have been 'necessary or proper for the attainment of justice' in the case of a plaintiff (or the defending of his rights by any other party). The Rule accordingly requires that an expenditure of a type which it was reasonable to incur must be allowed. The extent of allowance must be on the level of that which is 'necessary or proper' in order to have his case duly presented. It is not for a Court charged with the merits or the determination of liability for costs to compensate for a perceived inadequacy in the operation of the Rule by awarding costs on an attorney and client scale so that a 'full indemnity' of more comprehensive scope is achieved than the one which the Rule-maker envisaged.”
Bowman NO v Avraamides & Another 1991(1) SA 92 (W)
at p.95
[26]   The common law position regarding the fees which an attorney charges his client for professional services is that the attorney is allowed to recover from his client “even certain costs, such as luxuries that the client has agreed to pay for, that are not recoverable by that client from another party to litigation with him.”  This is the scale properly called attorney and own client (
Aircraft Completions Centre (Pty) Ltd v Rassouw and Others 2004 (1) SA 123
(W)
at para. [103] (1)).  The law regarding attorney and own client is trite as stated in
Hawkins v Gelb and Another 1959 (1) SA 702 (WLD)
at 705:
“Attorney and client costs are the costs that an attorney is entitled to recover from his client for the disbursements made by him on behalf of his client, and for the professional services rendered.  These costs are payable by the client whatever the outcome of the matter in which he engaged the attorney services, and are not dependant upon any award of costs by the court.  In the wide sense it includes all the costs that the attorney is entitled to recover against the client on taxation of his bill of costs, but in the narrow and more technical sense, the term is applied to those costs, charges and expenses as between attorney and client ordinarily the client cannot recover from the other party.”
[27]   Though, conceptually, the two scale of costs are different, for practical purposes, there is no difference between them on taxation. There is a good authority for this position in
Aircraft Completions Centre
case above at para. [100] (5-6):
“5….When an order that one party is to pay another’s costs taxed as between attorney and
own
client’ is made in preference to an order that the costs be taxed as between ‘attorney and client’… another limitation is at once encountered.  It is the limitation imposed by our own law as stated in
Nel
: when one party is ordered to pay costs of another taxed as between attorney and client, justice, as defined by the decision in
Nel
obliges the Taxing Master to disallow certain of the costs that the costs creditor may be obliged to pay his own attorney.  The costs to be disallowed include, in particular the costs of luxuries that the costs creditor may have bound himself by contract to pay his own attorney.  In terms of the decision in
Enslin v Gallo,
such costs must be taxed off even when the order is that one party should pay the costs of another taxed between ‘attorney and own client’.  The latter formular does not enable a costs creditor to circumvent the requirement of justice to the costs debtor, as defined in
Nel.
An ‘attorney and own client’ costs order relating to a bill to be paid by one party to another is therefore no more than a direction, to tax the bill on the ‘intermediate basis recognised in
Nel.
6. Therefore, for practical purposes, there is no difference between
an order that one party should pay the costs of another taxed as between ‘attorney and own client’; and
an order that one party should pay the costs of another taxed as between ‘attorney and client’ in accordance with
Nel.”
[28]   Although in the attorney and own client taxation the attorney is entitled to recover from his client even luxuries the latter has agreed to pay, the approach is different once the losing party is ordered to pay the costs of the other party. The over-arching principle of our law is that the taxing master should not allow an item in a bill of costs that will impose an unjust liability on a costs debtor (
R H Christie Incorporated v Taxing Master
case above:
see Aircraft
Completions Centre (Pty) Ltd v Rossouw and Others
case above at para. [108]
).
The costs of luxuries that the costs creditor may have bound himself by contract to pay his own attorney falls into this category. Another example of an aspect of attorney and client relationship which will certainly impose unjust liability on the costs debtor is when the attorney would have overreached his client
.
This was stated in
R H Christie Incorporated
case above at para. 56 where the court stated that:
“In relation to an attorney and own client bill the law is settled.  As far as expenses are concerned, that which has been specifically agreed, expressly and impliedly, may be recovered.  That too, is qualified.  A taxing master has a discretion to disallow certain expenses where an attorney has overreached his client or has been negligent or mala fide.  Overreaching means, in the context of an attorney and client relationship, inter alia, the extraction of a fee that is unconscionable or excessive …”
[29]   The court in
Mkuyana v Road Accident Fund 2020(6) SA 405 (ECG)
at para. [25] persuasively stated:
“The relationship between a client and his attorney is that of principal and agent based on a contract of mandate.  The attorney is entitled to be remunerated for his services.  His charges may be agreed in advance or they are the usual or normal fees due for the work actually performed.  Irrespective of whether the attorney’s fees are agreed, the fee charged must be reasonable.  Reasonableness is the standard against which the attorney’s fees must ultimately be measured.  Based on considerations of public policy the court retains the right to decide what is a fair and reasonable remuneration would be.  A fee that is unreasonable cannot validly be recovered, and a fee agreement that authorises an attorney to charge an unreasonable fee that amounts to overreachment, will be unreasonable and consequently unenforceable.  “An agreement would be unreasonable if, for instance, it authorised unprofessional fees or work or expenses which are unreasonable or unnecessary…“An unprofessional fee is a fee that constitutes overreachment, that is”, the extraction by an attorney from his client, by the taking by the former of undue advantage in any form of the latter, of a fee which is unconscionable, exercise or extortionate, and in so overreaching, his client that attorney would be guilty of unprofessional conduct.””
[30]
Approach to an order of costs on the scale as between attorney and client and own client.
In the present case the order of costs made by the court made it clear that the parties agreed that whoever ends up as the winner should be awarded 50% of the costs taxed as between attorney and client scale.  It is apposite therefore at this point to refer to the rule on tariff of fees for attorneys to elucidate the role of the taxing master when faced with an order of costs on attorney and client scale, especially as in this case where the order was a result of the private treaty between the parties.  Rule 56(5) of the old rules provides that:
‘The tariff of fees for attorneys where costs have been awarded to a party on a party and party basis shall be set out in the Fifth Schedule hereto.  Provided that the taxing master shall be entitled in his discretion to depart from any of the provisions of the tariff in exceptional circumstances, where adherence to such provisions would be inequitable.”
[31]   Where parties, as in this case, have an agreement to pay costs taxed as between attorney and client it has always been an established approach not to depart from the tariff of fees set for the attorneys on a party and party basis except where the taxing master finds that exceptional circumstances exist to make such a departure.  The approach was stated in
Gross v Svirsky 1923 TPD 422
at 425:
“The Court has held that the scale of fees laid down as between party and party in the tariffs of attorney’s fees applies mainly as between party and party, and it does not necessarily apply as between attorney and client.  I cannot agree with counsel’s contention that because a bill of costs is one between attorney and client, the fees allowed should be more liberal; that they should be on a higher scale, merely because it happens to be a bill between attorney and client and not between party and party.”
[32]
The Court in
Aircraft Completions Centre (Pty) Ltd
case at para. [85] in
agreement with the above approach stated:
“The logic of this proposition is unassailable.  It applies with equal force to taxation of a bill payable by one party to another that is to be taxed as between attorney and client.  On taxation as between attorney and client, irrespective of whether it is an attorney’s own client, or the opposing party, who may be obliged to pay the amount taxed, more is required than the mere fact that it is taxation as between attorney and client before a departure from the tariff is justified. Rule 70 (5)(a) determines what more is required…”
[33]   The equivalent of Rule 70(5)(a) being discussed in the above extract is rule 184(b) of the new rules or rule 56(5) of the old rules.  In terms of this rule in order for the taxing master to depart from applying the party and party tariffs there should be exceptionality about the case where adherence to the tariff would cause inequity or as
Trollip v Taxing Mistress of the High Court 2018 (6) SA 292 (ECG)
at para.15
states, when an “oppressive” result would ensue upon strict adherence to the tariff. More that is required to justify departure from applying the applicable tariff was aptly stated in
Aircraft Completions Centre
case at paras. [86]-[87] where the court stated:
“[86] There need not be anything ‘extraordinary or exceptional’ about a case in which costs are to be taxed in as between attorney and client.  When there is nothing extraordinary or exceptional, or if adherence to the tariff would in any event be equitable, the Taxing Master will rightly adhere to the tariff.
Loots
is an example of such a case.
[87] On the other hand, a case in which the parties have agreed that one should pay the other’s costs taxed as between attorney and client, and a case in which such an agreement has been made an court of court, may, on consideration of the circumstances, turn out to have features that make it ‘extraordinary or exceptional’ within the meaning of Rule 70(5)(a) and one in which strict adherence to the tariff would be inequitable.  When that is shown to be the position, the Taxing Master will be free to depart from the tariff to the extent justified by the circumstances.”
[34]   When on the other hand the parties have not agreed on the attorney and client costs, but the court has ordered them to mark it’s a displeasure towards the conduct of a litigant in a case:
“it is generally a strong indicator to the Taxing Master that in the Judge’s view the costs debtor has conducted his case in such a way as to be likely to have caused the costs creditor to incur costs that ought to have been unnecessary and may not be recoverable on a taxation as between party and party.  Such a finding by the Judge would therefore often lead the Taxing Master to conclude that the case is indeed an extraordinary or exceptional one, … and that a consideration of the bill of costs will show that strict adherence to the tariff would be inequitable.”
Aircraft Completions Centre
case above
at para.[92].
[35]
Analysis.
Central to this review application, as already stated, are items 9, 20 and 35 represented by the cumulative amount of M550,000.00 described as disbursements for counsel’s professional fees.  As I stated earlier, disbursements are actual monies paid to third parties by the attorney towards provision of professional services on behalf of a client.  These are represented in a column on its own in a bill of costs.  Where these disbursements are professional fees of counsel, they should be supported by counsel’s issued account to the instructing attorney for services rendered.  In the present case there was no account issued to the respondent’s instructing attorneys by counsel for services rendered.  Where counsel has not issued an account to instructing attorney, those costs should appear on the column of fees in bill at the rate allowed by rule 55 of the old rules – equivalent rule 185 of the new rules- supported by a marked brief of the advocate (rule 55(3)). In terms of rule 55(7) - equivalent rule 185 (7) of the new rules - where the court has ordered a party to pay another costs on attorney and client basis, such fees are subject to the moderating effect of the taxing master’s exercise of discretion to allow fees of an advocate he/she considers reasonable having regard to the circumstances of the case including the fee the advocate has marked on his briefs.
[36]   In the present matter we are concerned about the bill of costs as between attorney and client. The taxing master based his decision to allow disbursements for professional fee based on bank generated proofs of payment which represented an amount to M397,840.00.  It is not clear where he got the amount of M550,000.00 from. There was neither an advocate’s issued account nor marked brief of advocate to back his decision.
[37]   I revert to his rather terse reasons for his decision to allow these three items on the bill of cost.  In the so-called statement of the case, he states that:
“On the 5
th
August 2025, amongst other previous days; Mr Solomon (Representative of Adams Attorneys) and Mr Rasekoai (Attorney for the Respondents) appeared before me for the taxation of a Bill of costs.  The court had awarded the Applicants, costs on attorney and client scale, 50% thereof.
We attended items on the bill one by one and the parties agreed on most items excepts for a few that were taxed off.  However, Respondents’ attorney objected to disbursements of professional fees citing that they were too high.  Further he advised that I confine myself with the following consideration;
complexity of the matter
experience of the legal practitioner
whether the legal practitioner is an attorney or counsel
the tariff prescribed by law
when exercising my discretion whether or not to grant professional fees; I had the above considerations at the back of my mind one has learnt through experience that attorney and client costs are not a blank cheque; they must be reasonable, necessary and properly incurred however they are assessed more liberally than party and party costs…
In conclusion, on the 22 August 2025, I allowed the costs which were the subject of the objection by Mr. Rasekoai after having ensured that the costs were genuine (not fabricated or altered) and that they were supported and authentic documentation.  Furthermore, that they exceeded the amount awarded when halved.”
[38]   From the above excerpt it is evident that the taxing master did not exercise his discretion judicially; he either disregarded factors he should have considered or has acted on wrong principle in the following manner:  Based on the principles articulated above the fact that a party has been awarded costs on attorney and client scale is no license for the taxing master to allow fees liberally without adhering to the party and party tariffs as he seems to have thought. More is needed to allow fees liberally. The taxing master has to follow the tariff provided under rule 56(5) which provides that tariff of fees for attorneys where costs have been awarded on a party and party basis shall be allowed provided where in the exercise of his discretion he is entitled to depart from the provisions of the tariffs where exceptional circumstances exist, where adherence would cause inequity.  As already stated, there is nothing exceptional about an award of costs as between attorney and client in this case. In the present case, an award of costs as between attorney and client was made based on an agreement between the litigants. The taxing master should have determined which aspect of the case warrants departure from the tariff of costs as between party and party.  He does not appear to have been alive to these principles and approaches to taxation.
[39]   It is trite that an attorney must be remunerated for his services.  Whether the charges are agreed with a client in advance or they are for work actually done they must pass the reasonableness test. I agree with Mr Rasekoai that there has been overreachment in this case. As already seen a fee agreement that authorises an attorney to charge unreasonable fee that amounts to overreachment will not be enforced by the court
(
Mkunyana v Road Accident Fund
case
above at para.[26].  The present matter concerned an interlocutory application for a
mareva
injunction over a monetary claim of M1,330,000.00.  For counsel’s fees to amount to half the claim in the main case is unreasonable and unnecessary.  It is not clear whether the taxing master addressed himself to the tariffs applicable to advocates taking into account the complexity of the matter or the experience of counsel, other than to contend himself with merely saying that he allowed the costs
“after ensuring that the costs were genuine (not fabricated or altered) and that they were supported and authentic documentation.  Furthermore, that they exceeded the amount awarded when halved.”
[40]   With all due respect to the taxing master, what he states in the last line makes no sense at all.  It is also unintelligible. He seemed to have thought that proofs of payment which were provided were enough and nothing more was required of him, but as already seen he ought to have determined whether the fees were reasonable and necessary based among others on the advocate’s fees marked on his brief.  It does not appear that the advocate’s brief with marked fees, among others, was relied on by him.  Other than for him to record that he was alive that to the fact that he had to determine the reasonableness of the fees, it does not appear that he ever conducted that exercise because if he did, he would not have allowed the charges in the manner he did. They are unreasonable. All the above serve to highlight that the taxing master’s decision is reviewable.  He did not exercise his discretion judicially.  The application for an interdict was not complicated; it was a run-of-the-mill matter.  The court had directed that half the costs be allowed but he does not seem to have done so, at least evidently.
[41]   Mr Rasekoai had invited this court to consider that the applicable tariffs of fees for attorney and client are the repealed tariffs under schedule to Law Society Act, 1983 (repealed by Law Society Rules, 2012), through interpretative process.  In light of the discussion above there is no need to embark on that exercise.  There is no
lacuna
in our law as regards applicable tariffs when an order of costs as between attorney and client is made.
[42]   In the result the following order is made:
The matter is remitted to the Taxing Master to do taxation in line with the principles articulated in this judgment.
The applicant is awarded the costs of this review.
___________________________
MOKHESI J
For the Applicant:        Mr M. Rasekoai from RARALE Attorneys.
For the Respondent:     Advocate Makara from Makara and Monethi Attorneys.
